fields.description:
# S5 — Trusted-proxy passthrough verifier

## Why
Many organizations terminate authentication at an edge proxy / gateway (oauth2-proxy, API
Gateway, ALB) and want the backend to trust the already-authenticated identity — and it is the
only in-scope path for identity systems that are not token issuers (e.g. GitHub-user via
oauth2-proxy). This verifier lets rebar delegate auth to that fronting proxy safely.

## What
Add a **trusted-proxy** mode to `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py` (a small ASGI middleware plus a verifier),
selected via `mcp.auth_strategies` with params `proxy_secret_env` (the NAME of an environment
variable holding the proxy shared secret — no plaintext secret in the config file), `secret_header`
(default `x-proxy-auth`), `identity_header` (default `x-forwarded-user`), and a scopes source. The
middleware trusts the forwarded identity **only** when the proxy shared-secret header matches
(constant-time), strips any client-supplied identity header otherwise, normalizes header names
(so `X_Forwarded_*` cannot smuggle past), and hands the verified identity to the verifier which
mints an `AccessToken`.

## Approach & rationale
Implement the oauth2-proxy-blessed trusted-header pattern: (1) bind HTTP to loopback (S1 default)
so only the proxy can reach the server; (2) require a proxy shared secret (read from `proxy_secret_env`, never plaintext config); (3) strip/override any
inbound identity header unless the secret is valid. This is the standard, documented mitigation
for the forwarded-header-spoofing class (oauth2-proxy CVE-2026-40575 / CVE-2025-64484). A tiny
ASGI middleware is the right seam because forwarded identity lives on request headers, which the
token-only `verify_token` seam does not see.

## Scope
The trusted-proxy verifier + its header-guard middleware only, on the S2 seam. Subset of the epic.
Depends on S2. Independent of S3/S4/S6.

## Security
Boundary-crossing and itself a spoofing surface. Hard requirements: **loopback bind required**
when this mode is active (refuse a non-loopback bind without an explicit acknowledgment) — the
proxy is the only intended caller; a **constant-time** shared-secret check gates trust; any
client-supplied identity header is **stripped** unless the secret is valid; header-name
normalization defeats the underscore/case smuggling variant; fail-closed (no secret → no trusted
identity → 401).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Unit test (tier: unit — ASGI middleware exercised directly with crafted header scopes)
      asserts: with a valid `proxy_secret` header, the configured identity header yields an
      `AccessToken` (observable oracle: returned principal); without it, the identity header is
      **ignored/stripped** and the request is unauthenticated (401).
- [ ] Unit test asserts a client cannot spoof identity by sending the identity header directly
      (no proxy secret) nor via an underscore/case-variant header name (negative controls:
      `X-Forwarded-User` and `X_Forwarded_User` both stripped without the secret).
- [ ] Unit/integration test asserts enabling trusted-proxy mode with a non-loopback bind and no
      explicit acknowledgment refuses to start (observable oracle: startup fails with a clear
      message).
- [ ] Integration test (httpx `ASGITransport`) asserts an authenticated proxy request (secret +
      identity header) → 200 and a bare request → 401, end-to-end.
- [ ] `make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass.

## Compatibility
Additive, opt-in via `mcp.auth_strategies`. No change to other verifiers or the stdio path.
